An elementary observation.

Suppose that \(\{ c_{k}\}\) is a Cauchy sequence in \(C[a, b]\) under the \(L^{p}[a, b]\)-norm. Then for \([c, d]\subset [a, b]\text{,}\) \(\int_{c}^{d} c_{k}(x)\, dx\) is a Cauchy sequence in \(\bbR\text{;}\) and for any \(1\le q \le p\text{,}\) \(\Vert c_{k} \Vert_{L^{q}[c,d]}\) is a Cauchy sequence in \(\bbR\text{.}\) Furthermore, \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \int_{c}^{d} c_{k}(x)\, dx\) and \(\lim_{k\to\infty}\Vert c_{k} \Vert_{L^{q}[c,d]}\) remain the same if \(\{ c_{k}\}\) is replaced by an equivalent Cauchy sequence.
in-context
